claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 7 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start with: **Do you have any children that you're responsible for, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have children I'm responsible for. I have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll need to gather some details about each of your three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie - to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me start with some basic information: **How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 7 years old, Blake is 20, and Charlie is also 7.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Just to clarify about Blake who is 20 years old: **Is Blake currently in any approved education or training?**

(Child Benefit can continue for children under 20 if they're in approved education or training)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in education or training. He actually works full-time now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that about Blake.

Now I need to understand your living situation: **Do all three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie - currently live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. All three of them live with me here in the UK. I'm in England.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

One more important question: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these three children?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment

Based on the information you've provided, here is the outcome for your three children:

**✓ ELIGIBLE for Alex (age 7)**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Since Alex is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them, you meet all the requirements for Child Benefit.

**✗ NOT ELIGIBLE for Blake (age 20)**
Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. Child Benefit for children aged 16 and over only continues if they are under 20 AND in approved education or training. Since Blake is 20 years old and working full-time (not in education or training), they do not meet the eligibility criteria.

**✓ ELIGIBLE for Charlie (age 7)**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie. Since Charlie is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them, you meet all the requirements for Child Benefit.

**Summary:** You can claim Child Benefit for two of your children - Alex and Charlie. You would receive payments for these two children.

**Important note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, though you can still claim the benefit.

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. The claimant is responsible for Alex and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for this child. Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Alex meets all the eligibility criteria: they are under 16, live with the claimant, and the claimant is responsible for them. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 20 years old and works full-time. Blake is not in approved education or training. Under the Child Benefit rules, you'll normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. However, since Blake is 20 years old (not under 20) and is working full-time rather than being in approved education or training, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake. Additionally, the rules state that Child Benefit stops if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 7 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. The claimant is responsible for Charlie and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for this child. Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Charlie meets all the eligibility criteria: they are under 16, live with the claimant, and the claimant is responsible for them. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children: Alex (7), Blake (20), and Charlie (7). All live with the claimant in the UK and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie as they are both under 16 and live with the claimant. However, the claimant is not eligible for Blake because Blake is 20 years old (not under 20) and is working full-time rather than being in approved education or training. Child Benefit for children 16 and over only continues if they are under 20 AND in approved education or training, which Blake is not.

Alex meets all the eligibility criteria: they are under 16, live with the claimant, and the claimant is responsible for them. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 20 years old and works full-time. Blake is not in approved education or training. Under the Child Benefit rules, you'll normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. However, since Blake is 20 years old (not under 20) and is working full-time rather than being in approved education or training, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake. Charlie meets all the eligibility criteria: they are under 16, live with the claimant, and the claimant is responsible for them. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.

The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie as they are both under 16 and live with the claimant. However, the claimant is not eligible for Blake because Blake is 20 years old (not under 20) and is working full-time rather than being in approved education or training. Child Benefit for children 16 and over only continues if they are under 20 AND in approved education or training, which Blake is not.
